data.title: Rio Tinto's Hidden Catalysts For Long-Term Growth
data.description:
Rio Tinto (NYSE:RIO) shares have been relatively stable over the past 10 years. However, the dividend yield is high at 7.2% which makes the total return almost equal to that of the S&P500. The balance sheet has strengthened considerably in recent years but earnings remain volatile.

Rio Tinto has an interesting history because it has been around since 1873 and takes its name from the red-colored river 'Rio Tinto'. Now (some 150 years later) it is one of the largest mining companies in the world, and responsible for metals such as iron ore, copper and aluminum. However, now Rio Tinto is facing a challenging market and geopolitical uncertainty. Does that make Rio Tinto an attractive buying opportunity?

Growth Opportunities and Risks

Rio Tinto generates the majority of its revenue from iron ore, which is mainly exported from Western Australia to China. However, demand from the Chinese property market (which is a large consumer of steel) has weakened. This has led to lower iron ore prices and pressure on Rio Tinto’s margins. But there are also positive developments. The development of infrastructure worldwide and the energy transition can create a high demand for these raw materials. This could improve Rio's prospects if it succeeds in delivering projects such as Simandou, Resolution, Winu, the acquisition of Arcadium Lithium and the like.

However, the sUnited States has imposed tariffs on metals imported from China to reduce dependence on Chinese suppliers. This could have both negative and positive impact for Rio Tinto. For example, the Resolution copper project in Arizona (which has gained traction under Trump) could benefit from demand for U.S. mining operations. But trade tensions could also cause commodity price volatilty which can put pressure on margins.

Rio Tinto is still attractive due to its strong balance sheet, which gives the company ample fuel for investment such as the Simandou iron ore project in Guinea. This highly profitable project will produce high-quality iron ore and is ideal for the production of quality steel. Simandou could therefore become a new source outside of Australia. Rio is also investing in copper through projects such as Resolution in Arizona and the Winu copper/gold project in Western Australia, where Rio has a partnership with Japan’s Sumitomo Metal. And also, Rio Tinto will operate in the lithium market with the acquisition of Arcadium Lithium, which is essential for EVs and batteries. This diversification will reduce Rio's dependence on iron ore and could create more stable revenue streams.

Mixed Financial Numbers But Steady Outlook

Last year Rio Tinto had a mixed year. Revenue remained stable at around $54 billion, while EBITDA declined slightly to $23.3 billion due to lower iron ore prices. However, net income remained strong at $11.6 billion, helped by several one-off benefits such as favorable currency effects and tax benefits.

What I find particularly strong is Rio’s strong balance sheet with low net debt of just $5.5 billion, which equates to less than 0.3x EBITDA. This was different years ago, when net debt/EBITDA was 3x times. The current strong balance sheet offers opportunities to continue projects such as Simandou and Resolution. In addition, credit rating agencies give Rio Tinto strong credit ratings which ensures access to financing on favorable terms.

In the first quarter of 2025, Rio Tinto faced setbacks due to extreme weather conditions in Australia, resulting in lower iron ore deliveries. As a result, Rio expects annual production to be at the low end of its forecast. However, there were also positives: copper production from the Oyu Tolgoi mine in Mongolia grew strongly while bauxite production even reached a record high. This shows that Rio’s diversification strategy is slowly but surely starting to pay off which could provide stability in the longer term.

China remains a key growth market for Rio Tinto, accounting for around 57% of revenue. Although the real estate segment has weakened, Chinese infrastructure incentives could support metals demand. Rio Tinto expects 2025 to be a transition year with clear growth from 2026 onwards thanks to new production capacity.

Dividends And Stock Valuation

Rio Tinto is known for its attractive dividend policy, with a dividend payout of typically 60% of earnings. However, earnings have fluctuated in recent years resulting in variable dividend payments. Rio currently pays $4.35 per share which equates to an excellent dividend yield of around 7.2%. This makes the stock particularly attractive to investors seeking high dividend income, despite the cyclical nature of the commodities sector.

In terms of share valuation, Rio Tinto looks attractive with a P/E ratio of 8.7x and an EV/EBITDA of 6x. Both the P/E ratio and the EV/EBITDA are below those of peers such as BHP (P/E ratio of 14x and EV/EBITDA ratio of 6x). The market apparently sees little value in Rio Tinto, probably due to macroeconomic uncertainties.

Conclusion

Rio Tinto is currently facing a period of temporary headwinds, but also has strong catalysts for growth and diversification. The iron ore market remains challenging in the short term, but Rio has nice projects such as Simandou, Resolution Copper and the recent acquisition of Arcadium, which offer growth and diversification.

Its financial health remains steady with a strong balance sheet and a high dividend yield. For investors confident in the long-term demand for essential metals and minerals, Rio Tinto is currently an attractive investment. Looking at the bigger picture, I see a favorable risk/reward ratio and on that basis I recommend the stock as a Buy.

data.title: JQUA: Quality ETFs Offer Greater Prospects For Market-Beating Returns
data.description:
JPMorgan U.S. Quality Factor ETF (NYSEARCA:JQUA) is one of the best investment options in the quality factor category. The fund's approach of selecting stocks based on their earnings quality and shareholder return enabled it to outperform the broader market indices over the years.

JQUA's portfolio is diversified with less than 2.5% concentration in any position. Its portfolio also represents a high quality combination of growth and value stocks. The fund's expense ratio is lower than peers while the dividend growth is robust. Overall, JQUA provides an opportunity for moderate risk-tolerance investors to generate market-beating returns over the long term. I initiate coverage of JQUA with a buy rating.

Its peers, such as iShares MSCI USA Quality Factor ETF (QUAL) and Invesco S&P 500® Quality ETF (SPHQ), use various stock selection strategies to generate healthy risk-adjusted returns. However, JQUA's diversified portfolio, solid price momentum, healthy dividend growth and a low risk factor makes it a worth considering ETF than peers.

JQUA Can Generate Market-Beating Returns for Modest Risk Tolerance Investors

Unlike the sector, index, growth or value ETFs, quality-focused exchange traded funds offer access to the high quality companies with an objective of beating broader market indices over long term. These funds use factor-based stock screening strategies, such as fundamental analysis, bottom up approach, and earnings growth power to create their portfolios. In factor investing, fund managers believes that certain characteristics can help in generating market-beating returns over the long term. In addition, they rebalance their portfolios quarterly, or semi-annually to align them with market conditions.

In the case of JQUA, the fund designs its portfolio by selecting companies with well-established business models, strong profitability and the potential to increase shareholder return. With this strategy, its portfolio is composed of 275 value and growth stocks. In addition to a large number of holdings, JQUA's portfolio appears well-diversified with no holdings exceeding 2.50% of its total portfolio weight. Its top 10 stocks represent 19.25% of the overall portfolio. Furthermore, its portfolio is spread across all eleven sectors.

The technology sector represents 34% of its portfolio weight. A healthy exposure to the technology sector increases its potential to capitalize on tech-driven bull runs. Whereas, the financials, consumer discretionary and health care stocks mitigate the risk factor in tech-driven downtrend. Furthermore, stocks from the industrials, consumer defensive, energy and communication sectors increase the diversification factor.

From the technology sector, its top holdings include NVIDIA (NVDA), Microsoft (MSFT), Meta Platforms (META), Apple (AAPL), Alphabet (GOOG) (GOOGL), Palantir (PLTR), Salesforce (CRM) and ServiceNow (NOW). All these are the high quality companies with well-established business models and strong revenue generation potential.

Instead of relying on large banks, the fund's financials stocks mainly belong to services, insurance and investment industries. For example, Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.A) (BRK.B), Visa (V), BlackRock (BLK), S&P Global (SPG) and Marsh & McLennan (MMC) are its largest holdings from the financials sector.

Similarly, the high quality companies, including Johnson & Johnson (JNJ), AbbVie (ABBV), Merck (MRK) and Abbott Laboratories (ABT) and Gilead Sciences (GLD), are its top holdings form the health care sector. According to the FactSet data, the health care sector is expected to be a top contributor to the S&P 500 earnings growth in 2025. Meanwhile, the consumer discretionary and consumer staples sectors are popular for offering a steady share price and dividend growth over the long term.

JQUA's portfolio strategy seems to be working given its market-beating returns in both bull and bear markets. Its total returns outperformed the S&P 500 in the recent years. For example, its share price rallied 107% in the past five years compared to the S&P 500's 105% increase. With a dividend yield of 1.40% and dividend growth rate of 7.6%, its total returns soared to 123% in the past five years, up from the S&P 500's 108%. The fund paid dividends in the past seven consecutive years.

In addition, JQUA's shares are trading at cheap valuations compared to the S&P 500 and high beta growth category. Its shares are currently trading around 20x trailing and 18x forward earnings. The fund's low valuations are attributed to its portfolio combination of growth and value stocks.

In-line with my fundamental analysis, JQUA earned a buy rating with a quant score of 3.80. A higher price momentum, low expense ratio, strong liquidity and a lower risk factor helped it receive a buy rating. The strong quant grades on its momentum and risk factors vindicates my opinion about its potential to generate healthy risk-adjusted returns.

Where do the Peers Stand?

iShares MSCI USA Quality Factor ETF and Invesco S&P 500® Quality ETF are quality-focused ETFs. QUAL uses five factors for stock selection, including value, momentum, volatility, size, and quality. Its portfolio is composed of 126 high quality growth and value stocks. Its sector diversification is also high. The technology sector make up 31% of its portfolio weight while financials, health care and consumer cyclicals represent 15%, 11% and 10%, respectively.

The ETF received a hold rating based on the Seeking Alpha quant system. Compared to the JQUA, its share price momentum is lower and risk factor is higher. In addition, its dividend yield hovers below 1% and dividend growth rate in the past five years was 1.7%. Its expense ratio is 0.15%. QUAL has a strong liquidity with assets under management of $56 billion and average shares volume of 1.84 million.

Whereas, SPHQ outperformed QUAL and JQUA based on the total returns in the last twelve months and three years. The fund also received a high quant score of 3.94 with a buy rating, according to the Seeking Alpha quant analysis. The fund selects stocks based on the three fundamentals: return on equity, accruals ratio and financial leverage ratio. Its portfolio is composed of 102 growth and value stocks. SPHQ's portfolio exposure to the high beta stocks from technology sector is lower compared to its peers. Its top holdings are Visa, Mastercard (MA), Netflix (NFLX) and Johnson & Johnson.

Final Thoughts

Quality-focused ETFs appear to be attractive investment options for investors who seek to beat the market while limiting risk factor. JPMorgan U.S. Quality Factor ETF is one best investment options amid its diversified portfolio of growth value and stocks. Its low expense ratio and solid dividend growth makes it a solid long term investment. On the other hand, QUAL and SPHQ are also the great quality-focused ETFs.

data.title: Wall Street Roundup: UnitedHealth, Retail, Tech, And Tariffs
data.description:
Listen below or on the go on Apple Podcasts and Spotify

UnitedHealth and insurance inflection point (0:30). Tech names have been very headline dependent (5:55). Cisco's growth factors (10:10). Walmart set tone for upcoming retail earnings (11:10). Shopify's interesting earnings (13:20). Coinbase, S&P 500, Bitcoin (14:45). CPI biggest macro news (17:05).

Transcript

Rena Sherbill: Brian Stewart, our director of news. And for those that are just checking in for the first time, Brian and I do a weekly roundup every Friday recorded on Thursday afternoons. Here we are for another week. Brian, welcome back.

Brian Stewart: Great to be here.

RS: Summarize what we've been seeing for the past week. Certainly a lot going on, I would say. I was thinking we start with UnitedHealth (UNH). We had a CEO resign. Anybody that's paying attention to the world, I would say, has seen what's going on with UnitedHealth a little bit.

But from an investing perspective, what are you seeing from UnitedHealth?

BS: Yeah. I think it's interesting to look at the stock narratively. So starting with the high profile murder of its insurance group head, moving forward from there, we saw the company have earnings recently where it missed expectations, this had the stock going down and then, this week we had the abrupt departure of its CEO, Andrew Witty, also withdrew guidance at that point.

There were signs in there that the company was suffering from higher medical care activity and higher medical costs.

Basically, its insurance customers are using medical services more than they expected, and those services are coming at a higher price than was expected. So that's obviously cutting into the bottom line.

And then the final blow this week has been reports of a DOJ probe for Medicare fraud by the company. So the stock is down 32% over the the past five days. That's going into today and then down another 15% on Thursday on reports of the DOJ probe.

If you're looking at this from a bearish philosophy, you could say this company is in in tatters. It's one piece of bad news after another. If you're looking at it from a bullish perspective, the CEO leaving marks day zero for the new UnitedHealth, and they can move forward from here.

The DOJ investigation will be what it is, but that's under the old regime. And we can, as an investing group, move forward from there.

The question, I think, for investors in general is how company specific is this? There are some signs that this is relevant only to UnitedHealth, but the market seems to think it has broader sector impacts.

There's some signs that the news flow especially has been very company specific, but the market seems to see implications for the rest of the sector. You see Humana (HUM) down 12%. You see (CVS) down 11%. You see Cigna (CI) down 9%. This is over a five day span.

So the market seems to think that the macro picture for medical pricing and and the upswing in usage of medical services is probably going to affect all the insurance companies.

RS: Would you say that insurance companies are at a inflection point a la the tobacco industry? Like, walking around Los Angeles let's say, it's clear that Luigi, the murder of the executive from last year, it's taken on this somewhat Che Guevara type of resonance with everyone.

Do you feel that the DOJ probe is reflective of that? Do you feel like the slide in the names is reflective of that? How much is culture affecting things and how further would you say or what's the best take that you've seen on on what's happening in the insurance names?

BS: I think you're asking a very important question, and I think it has broader implications about government regulation and the relationship between the public and private spheres in the sense that we often as investors and in the pro business camp think of regulation as always being detrimental to a sector.

The more a sector is regulated, the less growth is possible there. So let's keep government regulation as low as possible. But I do think there are times when regulation or a stronger relationship between government oversight and an industry can actually provide the basis for a more confident investing environment, just a more confident situation surrounding that sector.

And I agree we might be reaching that point with the insurance sector where the public opinion about it has become so sour that having some sort of reform on a broad scale, even a mild one, would be enough to help return some of the confidence that people have lost in the sector.

RS: What is your sense in terms of the guidance coming? Do you have a sense that will be coming in the next quarter?

BS: It kinda depends on the new CEO coming in and how long it takes to right the ship there.

Next quarter might be aggressive. They're gonna want to get some information out, so maybe more signposts and less specific numerical targets.

But I do think that, say, six months from now, we'll probably get a situation where the new CEO is starting to put their stamp on things and set the longer term priorities for the company.

RS: So sector wise, another sector I was looking to cover were all these tech names in the news, reflective of the market volatility, reflective of this meeting in Saudi Arabia. We had Sara Awad from Tech Contrarians on Investing Experts laying out all these tech names and and how she sees it going.

What are you seeing out of the tech space this past week? By the way, excuse me, but I didn't even mention the China tariffs, which is also part of this volatility and spike up this week. Just wanted to get that in there.

BS: Yeah, I was gonna throw that in there. You know, it's Thursday, so Monday seems a long time ago, but we did have a huge market rally Monday after the the Chinese tariff announcement was made.

And then I agree, rolling from that right into the Saudi Arabia tech industry, the tech names, especially the big ones have been very headline dependent this year. It's been taking the eye off of fundamentals and looking more towards the macro picture.

I think the general take, the consensus take among investors is that the AI boom provides a medium term at least to long term growth environment for many of these tech names.

And the pace of that is going to be decided by macro factors. The state of the economy, the regulatory environment, the general fiscal and monetary policy that's going on. It makes it so investors are taking their foot off the gas when they have bad headlines or suboptimal headlines and slamming their foot back on the gas when do you see a reversal of that?

So I think going forward, you're going to continue to see these names be very dependent on what happens out of Washington, what happens out of other capitals elsewhere in the world. So keep an eye on the tariffs.

The main headline from the tariff meeting was a 90 day pause while we talk some more. And oh, the end result of those talks are going to be very important in determining the near term stock price for these companies.

RS: Anything to look for along the way there? Are there any hints that we may take, or is it really just a wait and see game?

BS: It's interesting because, clearly, Donald Trump's negotiating process is to keep options open as long as possible. So I'm thinking about, as an example, the TikTok situation, which was also kind of kicking the can down the road.

And this tariff situation is another one where at another ninety days to the negotiation process and see what we get out of it. Eventually, the market's going to want to see a deal, a final here's how things are gonna be going forward and move out of the uncertainty.

I'm just not sure what framework that's going to be. We have a ninety day ticking clock now, so we'll see as we get closer to the end of those ninety days. But all signs point to the likelihood that that'll possibly be extended. At the end of that, there's no reason to believe that ninety day limit is going to be some sort of hard deadline where we're definitely going to see a resolution.

So I think as we're mapping out the next few months, I would look for signs that things are starting to get locked down, and that might come in the form of smaller tariff deals with other countries.

So China's obviously the white whale, but along the way, there's many guppies and minnows and marlins, whatever fish you wanna name, on the way to bring into the boat.

So I would look if there's a proliferation of smaller tariff deals that start to get done, that might be a sign that the administration writ large is ready to put some signatures on the dotted line.

RS: And earnings wise, we haven't seen a ton, but we saw some big names report this week. Where where do you think is a good place to start with earnings?

BS: Cisco (CSCO) is the biggest name, up 6% today after its earnings. It beat expectations, gave better than expected guidance.

One of the main growth factors was AI orders. So I think this is just another example of a company where the knock on effects of the AI revolution are starting to be seen in a wide variety of companies.

So if you kind of see Nvidia (NVDA) as the point of the spear, you start to see it come down away from that tip to the market rich writ large. I think as an investor, as you're looking down the line, you wanna be looking for other companies that have that next growth wave still left to go.

And then ask yourself, if you're an investor in Cisco, to what extent has this AI growth been baked in, and and how much is there still left to go?

RS: Walmart (WMT) reported today. Back to our Investing Experts podcast, we just had J Mintzmyer on this week talking about why he was short Walmart.

So what would you say about Walmart's report? Certainly, there was a lot of interest given the tariff talk and how they were gonna come out about that and in general how they looked. What did you see out of Walmart?

BS: Walmart provided a good starting pistol for a run of retail earnings that are coming out next week. So just to list a few of them, you got Home Depot (HD) on Tuesday, you've got Lowe's (LOW) coming Wednesday, and then through the week you have Target (TGT), Best Buy (BBY), Williams Sonoma (WSM), Macy's (M), Dollar Tree (DLTR), Gap (GAP). So a wide variety of retailers coming out soon.

Walmart's earnings set the tone for that. Just looking at the retail sector generally, it's down about 5% year to date.

I'm talking now about the (XRT) S&P retail ETF. So that's a good look at the overall sector down about 5% year to date, but that's the end result of a very volatile year. So it's currently sitting at about 75. It was above 80 as late as February 6 and then fell to around 62 in early April.

That was driven by tariff concerns. So at that point in early April, it was down about 22% year to date and has crawled itself back. It's up about 21% from its lows, but still down for the year as a whole.

So you see that the retail sector is very tied into people's vision of both the tariffs and consumer sentiment writ large, whether or not inflation is going to force consumers to pull back on their buying.

Walmart warned that consumers might see higher prices even at reduced tariff levels. So you see this worry that even if a deal is struck with China and any of the other companies that are integral to the supply chain for these retailers, you might not see a complete pulling away of those price concerns.

The retailers might have that headwind even in a positive environment. The kind of environment that might spark a rally for tech stocks might not be enough to drive gains for retail.

RS: And anything else to point out earnings wise that you saw?

BS: Shopify (SHOP), had earnings early this week. It was up 14% after those results. I think it's interesting to look at Shopify in the context of the Internet commerce upswing that we saw last week.

Last week we saw companies like AppLovin (APP), and Carvana (CVNA) see gains and Shopify played into that. So there's clearly appetite for consumers to be purchasing items online. It will be interesting to see if this is a process of stuffing the channel before higher prices come in.

Consumers are as aware of it as anybody that tariffs are looming and so might be getting purchases done earlier in the year with the hope that they can get things cheap now and not have to worry about it later. It'll be interesting to see whether this kind of growth that these companies are seeing can be sustained through the year.

For a company like Shopify specifically, the concern is, okay, this was good execution for this past quarter, but our valuation's already out of whack.

So there's a there's a couple headwinds facing Shopify specifically and then the retail Internet retail sector generally.

RS: Speaking of sustained growth and how much can be sustained and how much growth is in there, I think a good segue from that conversation is the crypto conversation.

We saw some increases in Bitcoin (BTC-USD). It passed $100,000 again.

We saw some news out of Coinbase (COIN). They reported last week, but they're also set to join the S&P 500, which feels like a nice noteworthy moment in time in terms of crypto getting into the conversation, the day to day conversation. What are you seeing out of the crypto space? What kind of takes are you seeing there?

BS: I think your point about Coinbase joining the S&P 500 is a good one in the sense that this is another point on the road towards broad acceptance.

We had talked about before that the main driving force for higher Bitcoin prices and higher crypto in general will be the broadening acceptance by both institutions in terms of putting their clients into these assets as investing vehicles and consumers in general using them day to day.

And so I think Coinbase becoming officially one of the 500 most respected companies in, the investing world is an excellent step forward for that.

So if you're a bull on Bitcoin, I think you can take heart in that progression. Bitcoin, as you pointed out, has been volatile this year. It's still up 51% since the election, but had a swoon in kind of the middle of the first half.

So reached around 109 and then slipped and then last week got above a hundred thousand for the first time since early February. So you're seeing it hover below its recent highs at this point.

So the question is, where is the driving force for the next leg higher going to come? If you're a bear, you'd say maybe we're topping off at this point and looking for more catalyst.

If you're a bull, I think you're hopeful that the crypto friendly Trump administration that was promised on election day and drove many of those gains in late 2024 and early 2025 will continue to provide an environment where that broader acceptance can take place.

RS: And macro speaking, what do you have your eye on? How are you seeing things play out? What should investors be focused on? And what do you think they were most focused on this past week?

BS: Well, I think in terms of macro picture, I think the trade deals, the communication between the US and the rest of the world is the broadest context for which stocks are trading now.

So I think more headlines in that area are probably going to move the market a lot. I think in terms of digging down a little bit and looking at the underlying economy, I think the biggest news this week has been the CPI. It came in relatively benign.

It showed a 2.3% annual rate of growth for April, which was moderating from March and below expectations of a 2.4 increase. It's also worth noting that the Fed's target for inflation is 2%, so this is getting pretty close to that.

However, there is some uncertainty in the fact that the core number, so that include that excludes food and energy, that number came in at plus 2.8%, so you're seeing sticky inflation on the core number even as the headline number comes down.

Also, the April number did not include the tariffs. And now that those have been paused ninety days, maybe that doesn't have as much of an effect as people were counting on.

But there's still gonna be a tariff impact, and we're still kind of waiting for that shoe to drop. And that's being shown in the probabilities for whether or not the Fed's going to cut rates in the near future.

So if you look at the the market and what it's expecting currently, it's pricing in a 92% chance of no cut at the June Fed meeting. That was already pretty much baked in that everyone was expecting June to be another hold and see what happens.

But if we look ahead to July, it's now a 61% chance of a no cut. That was 42% a week ago. So you're seeing July flip from majority chance that there was going to be a cut to a majority chance that there isn't going to be. And then September, now there's a 25% chance of no cut and that was at 11% a week ago.

So you continue to see the the prospect of a rate cut moving further and further down the horizon later and later into 2025. And while the majority of people are still expecting rate cuts in the relatively near future sometime this year, that keeps being pushed back, keeps getting further and further away.

The concern is that the underlying economy will need some sort of boost sometime in the near future. And if inflation remains sticky and that's exacerbated by tariffs, it might tie the Fed's hands. It won't be able to act as quickly as it otherwise would.

The housing market's gonna be in focus next week. There's new home sales, existing home sales are both coming out. Also, as I mentioned, Home Depot and Lowe's are reporting earnings, so you're gonna get a pretty good look at what the housing market looks like.

That's an interesting one. It's very rate sensitive. And as people are expecting rates to come down, I don't know how much that's permeated the general thinking of a home buyer.

But there might be the thought that if rates are coming down later this year, maybe I'll put off buying a house, locking in a rate until I can get a little bit less, the mortgage.

RS: I can tell you that empirically speaking, I went looking for nails this week to hang something on the wall, those kinds of nails. I went to so many different places that do not sell them, that should be selling them. They're off the shelves for various reasons. The only place where we could find them was Home Depot. I think that might be a a real nice indicator. I think people can extrapolate a lot from that data point.

BS: One thing I would say from that is I mean, that's sort of the the Walmart thing we were discussing last week where if you're one of these bigger companies, you just have better relationships with suppliers.

They might be more insulated because we think about it as, like, oh, no. Walmart is gonna be hit by these tariffs because they get all their products from China, but they might actually be more insulated than some of the smaller players just because they have more options in terms of leveraging their sources.

RS: To me, it's a similar conversation, although I don't mean to mix my metaphors, but what a lot of people say about, like, the ETF space in Bitcoin, they're only going for BlackRock. They're only going for Fidelity. Like, those big names. There is cache that is worth the brand name.

BS: Yeah. And if you're an AI person, maybe that speaks to NVIDIA. You think, okay. NVIDIA laid out, so let's move on, find something smaller. But there's something to be said for being the the largest player in a certain segment.

data.title: Synopsys: It Wouldn't Surprise Me If The FY2025 Guidance Was Lowered
data.description:
Synopsys Investment Thesis

Synopsys, Inc. (NASDAQ:SNPS) is a highly interesting company that is currently in a situation that is attracting a lot of attention. On the one hand, there is the upcoming merger with Ansys (ANSS), and on the other hand, the company is operating in the semiconductor market, which is one of the most important and widely discussed markets of the moment.

Therefore, it is not surprising that tariff negotiations could significantly impact this company and it will be interesting to see how they influence Synopsys's results and forward guidance. The next earnings call may even provide interesting insights into the general AI landscape.

Therefore, it would not surprise me if the FY25 guidance is lowered in the next quarterly results to be more conservative and avoid unpleasant surprises in the future.

SNPS Q2 Earnings Preview

Synopsys will present its results for the second quarter of 2025 on May 28 after the market closes. And the current forecast for Q2/2025 is that the revenue will be between $1.59 billion and $1.62 billion and the EPS will be between $3.37 and $3.42.

For FY25, the guidance is currently for revenues to be between $6.7 billion and $6.8 billion, which would correspond to a growth rate of 10-11%. For diluted EPS the guidance is to be between $14.88 and $14.96.

Unfortunately, the topic of tariffs, trade deficits and export restrictions came up shortly thereafter, and I believe they will affect Synopsys's business numbers in 2025. Although I believe the impact on the Q2/2025 numbers will not be too significant, I would not be surprised if they come in at the lower end of the guidance or even slightly below.

However, I believe that the guidance for fiscal year 2025 could be lowered because there is still a lot of uncertainty about how things will develop. In particular, since the current guidance assumed that the second half of 2025 would be better than the first. However, I don't think we can say that with full conviction anymore. This is because the planned revenue split of 45/55 could now turn out very differently if trade talks stall or flare up again.

Unfortunately, the guidance for 2025 was already lowered in Q4 of last year, which caused the shares to plummet by almost 12%. So if Synopsys were to lower the guidance again, it would not surprise me if the stock price dropped again. But since I assume that the problems regarding the trade deficits are temporary, I believe that Synopsys will be fine in the long run and that a decline could provide good entry points. As a matter of fact, I still think that Synopsys and Cadence (CDNS) will continue to dominate because both are years ahead of the competition.

If the Ansys and Synopsys deal is finalized this year, as it currently appears, I believe the merged company will become one of the most important in the AI industry. And because a preliminary agreement has been reached with the Chinese antitrust regulatory authority, I believe that the 2025 timeline is realistic for completing the deal.

It will also be interesting to see how the partnership with Intel (INTC) develops regarding the 18A process node. If Intel returns to its former strength—which is not guaranteed—this could be a deal that would positively impact Synopsys' sales.

When we compare the last revenue estimates with the actual revenue, we find that the revenue surprise was always very small. This suggests that Synopsys' management is excellent at sales forecasting. But since we are now dealing with an incredibly significant event with far-reaching consequences in terms of tariffs, I could well imagine that the estimates will be undercut, as this was something that was probably not taken into account in this form.

However, EPS surprises have always been relatively significant with beats in the mid single digits, so I believe the EPS guidance is typically set low to make surpassing it easier. Therefore, despite the negative effects of the trade restrictions, it seems realistic that the EPS will be similar to the guidance.

However, if Synopsys can maintain its revenue and earnings per share within the projected range despite significant uncertainty, it will be a testament to the effectiveness of its management and forecasting teams.

For example, J.P. Morgan assumes that the semiconductor industry's Q1 results will likely meet expectations, but FY guidance could be reduced by 15-25%.

Conclusion

Overall, I think it's realistic that the guidance for fiscal year 2025 could be lowered, simply because uncertainty is currently very high and in a situation like this, it's always wise to set the bar low so you can easily surpass it. Although this would likely cause short-term pain, the stocks should eventually recover due to a potential increase in future profits or sales.

This is particularly true given that negative news in connection with the merger could also cause the shares to come under pressure. Therefore, I think a conservative guidance would provide a margin of safety here.

data.title: Septerna: Deal With Novo Nordisk Is A Game Changer While It Still Trades Well Below Net Cash
data.description:
Septerna, Inc. (NASDAQ:SEPN) is an early stage biotech pioneering a new era of G protein-coupled receptor, or GPCR, oral small molecule drug discovery through its proprietary Native Complex Platform. The company received a big boost to its credibility and balance sheet after announcing an agreement with Novo Nordisk A/S (NVO) on Wednesday. The two companies will collaborate to potentially develop oral small molecule medicines for obesity and other cardiometabolic diseases.

The stock reacted positively, rising 51% on Wednesday and settling at just over $10.00 per share. However, that still leaves the stock trading below net cash post-deal, with one of the world's leading manufacturers of weight loss drugs providing a major sign of support to back up the company's novel platform. SEPN traded over $800 million in enterprise value upon its debut on the market several months ago, showing the initial excitement over its Native Complex Platform. Now investors can purchase the stock and get that same platform, with NVO's backing, essentially for free.

The global collaboration and license agreement between SEPN and NVO is to discover, develop and commercialize oral small molecule medicines for obesity, type 2 diabetes and other cardiometabolic diseases. The agreement calls for the commencement of four simultaneous research and development programs. Each of these programs enables SEPN to receive approximately $500 million in research, development, regulatory, and commercial milestone payments along with tiered royalties in the mid to high single digits upon successful commercialization. NVO will cover the expenses related to running these programs. SEPN will also receive an upfront payment of $195 million from NVO.

Septerna trades below net cash both before and after this deal

Novo reserves the right to terminate this deal at any time, but the $195 million payment is non-refundable. So even if the collaboration doesn't result in a commercial product, SEPN receives at least this amount. With 44.5 million shares outstanding, the $195 million payment equates to $4.38 per share in cash. Yet at $10.00, the stock is up a little over $3.00 on the news.

Before the deal was announced, SEPN was already trading materially below its net cash. As of December 31, 2024, the company had short-term cash and equivalents of $351 million and working capital of $344 million. In addition to that, it had $70 million in non-current marketable securities. Including this amount in the working capital balance leads to total net cash of $414 million, or $9.30 per share. Adding in the upfront payment of $4.38 per share and SEPN has $13.68 per share in cash, less any cash burn for the first five months of 2025. SEPN had a net loss of $72 million and an operating cash outflow of $67 million in 2024. At this pace, I estimate about $30 million has been spent so far in 2025, so net cash is likely around $13.00 per share.

Prior to the deal with Novo, the company expected to have enough cash runway into early 2028 while the current burn rate implies a cash runway of nearly six years. A three year cash runway would mean that the company likely expects a $120 million to $140 million burn rate going forward. That burn rate will likely be lower in 2025 then increase in the following years to pay for active trials. The Novo deal not only adds about two years worth of cash to SEPN's balance sheet, it likely also lowers the burn rate as Novo will be covering the expenses of four programs. As all of SEPN's pipeline is early stage, there will be substantial expenses undertaken until there is a viable commercial product with no guarantee of success. That being said, not only are investors getting the pipeline and the Novo agreement for free, they are being paid about $3.00 per share right now to take it.

Native Complex Platform and its pipeline

So what exactly are shareholders getting and why is NVO willing to pay as much as over $2 billion to secure a piece of it? SEPN's Native Complex Platform looks to bust the door wide open on drug discoveries related to GPCRs. GPCRs account for approximately 33% of all FDA-approved drugs, but about 75% of potential GPCR therapeutic targets don't have any drugs in the market. The inability to isolate GPCR proteins in their native functional form outside of a cell prevents drug discovery.

The Native Complex Platform is able to replicate the natural structure, function, and dynamics of GPCRs outside of cells at an industrial scale. This enables the company to apply the tools of drug discovery to GPCRs that up until now were impossible to reach. The end result is a potential deep portfolio of oral small molecule GPCR-targeted programs across multiple therapeutic areas. We can see the potential of this pipeline on the company's website:

Considering the history of success with FDA-approved drugs for GPCRs, investors should take this early stage pipeline more seriously than the average biotech in this highly speculative position. In addition to a focus on metabolic diseases, which will now be led by NVO, Septerna has a pre-clinical pipeline in endocrinology, immunology and inflammation. It is also evaluating drug candidates in neurology, women's health, cardiovascular and respiratory diseases. With such a wide net of early stage candidates, it's understandable why the burn rate is so high even before trials have begun. There may be a risk of lack of focus. But there is also an opportunity that further collaborations similar to the one signed with NVO may occur beyond the metabolic disease category. To me, this potential means that SEPN should be trading with a positive enterprise value. Not this far below net cash.

Conclusion: SEPN is a strong buy as I expect the stock to trade at least at net cash of $13.00 per share

SEPN is a demonstration of momentum and speculative hype making for an inefficient market. Three months after its IPO, the stock traded well above $20 with an enterprise value above $800 million for an early stage pipeline. The market was paying a high premium for the Native Complex Platform. Fast forward another few months and the stock is trading at less than half of that level, well below net cash with a deal with NVO. Occasionally the market's lack of logic on more obscure names provides a strong buying opportunity. SEPN is one such opportunity. I have purchased shares and a small amount of call options in anticipation that the stock will trade at or above its net cash of $13.00 per share.

data.title: Charter Communications Buys Cox In A Shrewd Deal
data.description:
Shares of Charter Communications, Inc. (NASDAQ:CHTR) have been an excellent performer over the past year, adding 55%, as the company’s solid free cash flow has helped to offset fears about cord-cutting. Investors got more good news on Friday with the stock jumping 3% on news that CHTR would be merging with Cox Communications. This will give the company increased scale, providing ongoing cost-savings opportunities, and the premium paid is quite small.

I last covered shares in April, upgrading CHTR to a “Buy.” Since then, the stock has added 18% while the market is up 12%. Given the news and its strong performance, now is a good time to revisit CHTR. I remain bullish.

The Cox deal is attractively priced

On Friday, Charter announced a deal to acquire Cox Communications for $34.5 billion. Cox is a private, family-controlled company, so financial performance history is limited. This deal values Cox at $34.5 billion, including the assumption of $12.6 billion in net debt. Cox will receive $4 billon in cash, $6 billion in a convertible note, and $11.9 billion of outright stock. Upon conversion of the note, Cox will own 23% of the combined company. Charter’s CEO will remain CEO of the company while Cox’s CEO will be Chairman of the board. There will be restrictions on Cox that limits the family’s ability to add more shares. The company will rename itself Cox from Charter, but Spectrum will be used as the consumer-facing brand.

Charter is paying 6.44x Cox’s 2025 forecast EBITDA, which matches its own valuation based on a $356 share price that it has averaged the past 60 days. With shares having rallied, the deal values Cox for about $3 billion more, but it remains a multiple-neutral deal with Charter’s multiple having expanded as the stock has rallied. Given these are similar businesses (as I will discuss below), the acquirer usually has to pay a premium, so doing a deal at the same EBITDA multiple is quite attractive.

While the Cox family is not receiving a premium multiple, the benefit for them from this deal is that their net worth will now be substantially more liquid as a holder of CHTR stock vs owning a private company. From an estate planning perspective, that is preferable, and by doing most of the deal in equity, their tax impact is much lower than if they sold the company outright. As such, both sides can see some accretion: CHTR by not paying a premium and Cox in terms of improving the family’s long-term liquidity and tax position.

This is the second deal announced in a short period of time. Last November, Charter announced a deal to acquire Liberty Broadbands (LBRDA). As I discussed in April, that deal is purely financial as LBRDA is a tracking stock that owns Charter shares. As such, there is no integration risk, and I see about ~$9 per share of accretion from its terms. Both deals are timed to close in June 2027.

Two years to close a transaction is longer than I like, as both companies are stuck in “limbo” for some time. I am more comfortable with the delay given it is an equity deal rather than primarily a cash deal, as that does keep interests aligned. This deal will likely receive some regulatory scrutiny, lengthening the time to close. It was interesting that the press release literally says the deal “puts America first by returning jobs from overseas.” I do not wish to make a politically normative statement, but it is clear the company is already messaging this deal in a way to garner more favor from the current Administration and assure approval, which is a shrewd tactical move.

There is at least ~$850 million of EBITDA synergies

Importantly, there is very little overlap between the two networks, which should limit regulatory concerns. It is notable how much larger Charter’s network is geographically. Cox has 20% of Charter’s customers but a much smaller footprint. That is because it operates in much denser markets, around DC, Connecticut, Atlanta, New Orleans, and elsewhere.

In 2024, Charter had a 41.0% EBITDA margin while Cox was 41.2%, so it is not like Cox has substantial bloat in its cost structure, which Charter can cut. Cox also less video exposure, meaning there is less future cord cutting risk. Video can be lower margin, so adjusting for this, Charter is likely a slightly leaner organization. I do also see revenue synergy opportunities. About 1/3 of Charter’s 31 million customers have a mobile line with Charter. In comparison, just 2% of Cox’s 6 million customers have a mobile line. This is the area where I see the greatest revenue opportunity, potentially about $600 million.

The combined company has about $40 billion of annual operating expenses. Aside from making Cox leaner, there will likely be duplicative back office and G&A functions, which can be eliminated. For instance, they will not need to retain two accounting departments. In theory, greater scale could improve negotiating for video costs with content companies, but I am cautious on the magnitude of savings. Still, even assuming just a 1-2% op-ex overlap, there is $600 million of potential cost synergies.

Combined with some mobile benefits, I see about $850 million of combined EBITDA upside and about $625 million of incremental free cash flow. This is about 3% accretion, which roughly matches today’s share price increase. I also view my synergy assumption as conservative, especially as it assumes no benefits from negotiations with content providers.

Charter results are also coming in as hoped

It is important to note that CHTR is performing well on a stand-alone basis. In the company’s first quarter reported on April 25th, Charter earned $8.42, which beat estimates by $0.05. Charter reported a 2% decline in customer relationships to 31.4 million. Mobile lines remain the standout, rising 26% while video was down 7% and interest was down 1.6%. CHTR added 500k mobile lines in Q1. With video losing customers, we see CHTR continue to lose 3-product penetration, which is likely to persist indefinitely.

Total revenue was flat at $13.7 billion. Internet revenue rose by 2% with mobile up 34% and video down 8%. We continue to see Internet revenue outpace Internet customers, given price increases, which I believe is sustainable. I also continue to be encouraged by mobile revenue rising faster than mobile customers. Given positive pricing, EBITDA grew by 5% to $5.76 billion. EBITDA margins expanded 180bps to 42%, and CHTR generated $1.56 billion of free cash flow. This enabled $751 million of Q1 buybacks.

This deal will also reduce leverage

One concern about CHTR is its high debt load, but this deal will help to alleviate that risk. It will move its targeted leverage ratio down to 3.5-4x, it aims to achieve the mid-point 2-3 years post-closing. Charter’s pro forma Q1 leverage was 4.2x with $95 billion of debt. Cox is just 2.2x. As such, the combined company will be 3.9x. Because Cox is a lower-debt company, this deal immediately reduced Charter’s leverage, and Charter will incrementally reduce leverage thereafter. Lower leverage should help reduce the premium it pays to borrow, which will be especially important in the 2028-2031 period when it has substantial maturities.

With further deal upside, I remain a buy on CHTR

Overall, I view this deal positively. CHTR expands its presence in high density regions of the country without having to pay a premium and also deleverages its balance sheet as a result. There is likely to be at least $850 million of EBITDA accretion with the potential for upside beyond $1 billion. I saw $5-5.5 billion of free cash flow over the medium-term, assuming $9 billion of normalized cap-ex spending, or $36 per share. Pro forma for this deal, I now see about $38-40 per share of free cash flow, starting in 2028.

That leaves shares with a long-term free cash flow yield of about 9.3%. I see a terminal value of about $480-500. I haircut this by about $25 to reflect the 3-year ramp to this level of free cash flow, given higher cap-ex today, for a fair value of $465. This is up from $430 previously, reflecting benefits from this deal and solid standalone results.

CHTR shares have another 9% upside to my fair value. This leaves them on the cusp between a buy and hold in my view. At this time, I am remaining a “Buy,” given my fair value is using a very conservative synergies assumption and a lower leverage level could lead to a higher multiple over time. Still, the pace of outperformance is likely to slow. CHTR’s rally is justified, and this deal is good news. As such, I remain a buyer, but if shares move past $450, I would start to take some off the table out of prudence.

data.title: Bitdeer Q1 Earnings Review: Ambition Meets Execution Risk
data.description:
Bitdeer (NASDAQ:BTDR) is one of the favorite miners to emerge in the last Bitcoin (BTC-USD) market cycle. I initiated coverage on Bitdeer about a year ago (June 2024), around the release of last year's Q1 earnings. What caught my eye was the degree of vertical integration the “young” miner was showing - proprietary chips and mining rigs via the SEAL chips and SEALMINER line, as well as tailored solutions for fleet efficiency. Such level of in-house capability was too good to ignore, just coming out of the fourth halving two months prior. Mining efficiency was the top priority for every investor and miner at the time. What better way to ensure that than by being vertically integrated?

BTDR is still up 98% since initial coverage. Then, a follow-up coverage reiterating a Buy rating came in December last year. From the date of the follow-up coverage, BTDR is down 38%. Miners have faced correction along with the broader market so far this year, though things have now started to cool off, and a recovery is taking place among mining stocks. I think BTDR’s decline in particular was steeper because it had already enjoyed an outsized upside in 2024. As the next chart shows, BTDR printed more gains in 2024 than peers. In fact, by the end of last year, if we compare BTDR’s gains to those of other miners throughout the year, BTDR dwarfed them all, with 241% gain in the second half of 2024 alone (post halving).

Q1 2025 earnings are in. Just like peers, the results are a mixed bag. The top line was a beat, and the GAAP bottom line turned positive (though not from operations), operating income and margins were subpar. Revenue came in at $70.1 million, a 41% YoY decline. Gross profit was negative $6.3 million, showing margin pressure, while adjusted EBITDA came in at negative $36.8 million. Adjusted net loss was $89.8 million, despite the GAAP-reported net income of $409.5 million, which was largely driven by the non-cash derivative revaluation gain.

The drop in sales was across all segments of the company. Revenue from self-mining declined to $37.2 million from $48.4 million a year ago, attributed to the April 2024 halving and higher network hashrate. Cloud hash rate revenue collapsed to just $0.1 million from $18.1 million as contracts expired and machines were reallocated to self-mining. General hosting revenue fell to $9.6 million from $29.0 million. Membership hosting dropped to $16.3 million from $19.5 million a year ago. Revenue from sales of Bitdeer’s proprietary mining machines SEALMINER came in at $4.1 million.

One encouraging takeaway from the sales breakdown is that Bitdeer is recording actual revenue from SEALMINER. The company has allocated an ample amount of R&D resources to develop the SEAL chips and SEALMINERS. Q1 was no exception as the financials show.

As seen in the $153.7 million inventory level on the balance sheet, Bitdeer’s cash flow from operations was heavily impacted by working capital outflows tied to SEALMINER production, leading to $284 million operating cash outflow for the quarter. The balance sheet remains healthy though, with $215.6 million in cash and cash equivalents, $215.4 million in total borrowings, and $131.1 million in crypto holdings. But vertical integration (mainly manufacture of the SEAL chips and SEALMINERS) and pivot to HPC/AI, all happening at once, is coming at a high cost.

Bitdeer ended Q1 in a net cash burn, with total outflows from operations and investing activities reaching $357.6 million, only partially offset by $94.9 million in financing inflows. If this rate of cash burn persists into Q2 without a meaningful offset from revenue growth or at least external capital raises, Bitdeer would be forced to dip into its cash and crypto reserves to maintain liquidity, or at least embark on heavy dilution - $118 million was already raised in Q1 through ATM, $106 million raised in January and $12 million raised in February.

Some strategic initiatives undertaken in Q1 include the revision of infrastructure CaPex guidance from $340 to $370 million down to $260 to $290 million, partly due to a pause in Bitcoin mining-related construction at the Clarington Ohio site to focus more on HPC/AI. While this is strategically opportunistic given the growing demand for AI compute, it also indirectly functions as a move that reduces cash outflow in the interim, which as said earlier, has been heavy.

Operationally, Bitdeer continues to energize SEALMINER A1 and SEALMINER A2 machines, and as of the end of Q1 self-mining hashrate was 11.5 EH/s. That figure stands at 12.4 EH/s today as more SEALMINER A1 and SEALMINER A2 machines were energized last month. Bitdeer is rapidly scaling deployment of their proprietary rigs in-house. Bitdeer targets 40 EH/s by the end of this year. Current average miner efficiency at Bitdeer is 29 J/TH. At 29 J/TH, scaling with more efficient SEALMINERS better come soon and decisively because efficiency data from other miners as of Q1 end shows Bitdeer lagging in this aspect.

Fleet efficiency (J/Th)

Bitdeer

29 J/TH

CleanSpark (CLSK)

16.15 J/TH

Hut 8 (HUT)

20.0 J/TH

Riot Platforms (RIOT)

21.0 J/TH

I think Bitdeer Q2 efficiency will be better as the company has said they are taking advantage of the temporary 90-day suspension of U.S. tariffs on certain goods. Bitdeer is strategically shipping mining rigs from Southeast Asia to the U.S. On top of the in-house deployment, Bitdeer is taking the SEALMINER business line seriously. They intend to start manufacturing SEAL chips and SEALMINER machines in the U.S. This move is part of a broader strategy to mitigate risks associated with international trade tensions by localizing production.

Overall, Q1 shows both ambitions and headwinds (most related to spending). And I think the bull case for Bitdeer is still somewhat intact. Investors should note that execution risk has risen with HPC. Every miner’s HPC bet remains an unproven thesis until non-Bitcoin revenue steadily offsets BTC bear market cycles.

I’m inclined to reiterate a Buy here, but this is a very cautious buy, and should be for investors who believe that Bitdeer's SEALMINER or HPC revenue will meaningfully materialize by at most Q4 this year, and the 40 EH/s target will also be reached.

Bitdeer has ample power capacity currently online - 1098 MW capacity online and 1592 MW in pipeline, with over 700MW to go online between Q2 and Q4 this year - to pursue and quickly scale into HPC. That power capacity also underpins its mining capacity to the 40 EH/s target. Overall, Bitdeer remains one of the operationally attractive miners.

data.title: Nutex Health: The Stock Has Gotten Ahead Of Itself As Arbitration Revenue Normalizes
data.description:
Last month I wrote an article on Nutex Health Inc. (NASDAQ:NUTX) with a hold rating despite blowout earnings that suggested a much higher stock price. I felt that the big wins the company had experienced due to arbitration under the No Surprises Act left it with too many unknowns in the near future. Fast forward several weeks and the company's stock has more than doubled, including another 26% gain on Wednesday thanks to another set of strong earnings for Q1 2025. With another quarter in the books, I felt that it was a good time to provide an update on my opinion about the company.

Q1 results show arbitration drives the increase in performance yet again, but even when excluding it, the numbers are strong

Revenue increased an outstanding 214% for Q1 2025, growing to $211.8 million compared to $67.5 million for Q1 2024. $105 million in revenue was due to the arbitration process. Excluding this amount leads to $106.8 million in revenue, a very strong 58% growth over Q1 2024. Q4 2024 achieved $88 million in revenue excluding arbitration and a 26% growth rate over Q4 2023. So Q1 2025 achieved a material step up in operations both sequentially and when comparing year-over-year.

However, the $105 million arbitration win was a significant step down from the $170 million arbitration win seen in Q4 2024. That resulted in operating income of $72.2 million. That's a massive increase over Q1 2024's result of $1.5 million, but it's also significantly down from $114.2 million in operating income from Q4 2024. The end result is that EPS for Q1 2025 was $2.56. This is also down significantly from Q4 2024's result of $11.12 EPS.

The $105 million in arbitration revenue comprised of $60 million generated in Q1 with the remaining $45 million generated from prior periods. This compares to $69 million of the $170 million arbitration revenue for Q4 2024 being generated in quarter with the remaining $101 million generated in previous quarters. Note that in one of my bullet points in my previous article, I stated that:

Should the arbitration process in 2025 yield results that are half as lucrative as in 2024, NUTX is at risk of eroding all of its net income.

The 77% decline in EPS from a 32% decline in arbitration win in sequential quarters is proof of that point. Had the company not done so well in Q1 in its base operations before arbitration, this decline would have been even more pronounced. As time goes on, I expect prior period arbitration revenues to normalize as the company gets better at capturing them in the quarter they are generated.

In my previous article, I mentioned the bulging accounts receivable balance and relatively small operating cash flow compared to net income as two risk factors that investors would have to pay close attention to going forward under the arbitration-focused business model. I feel that NUTX made progress on the cash collection side of the arbitration process in Q1. Reviewing the operating cash flow for Q1:

NUTX achieved $51 million in operating cash flow for Q1, a record for the company. Note that $72 million in operating income generated this level of operating cash flow. This compares to $23 million in operating cash flow generated for all of 2024 on operating income of $131 million. The company is starting to convert its accounts receivable into cash. The A/R balance still increased $63 million from the end of 2024 to March 31, 2025, but I view the increasing operating cash flow compared to net income as a positive sign.

Conclusion: As the arbitration wins heads to a steady state, the stock price has gotten ahead of itself

On the Q1 conference call, management made reference to arbitration revenue heading towards a steady state (though not completely there yet). If this is the case, investors should expect results more in line with Q1 EPS than the $11.12 EPS achieved in Q4 2024 going forward. Analyst consensus has NUTX achieving $11.89 in EPS for 2025. That would result in NUTX trading at a 13x forward earnings multiple, below its sector median of 17x. This suggests another 25% to 30% upside. However, given the volatile nature of the arbitration wins and their leveraged influence on the bottom line, I think a discount to sector median is warranted.

NUTX has a marvelous business model and appears to be well managed but I feel like the stock has gotten ahead of itself. In my opinion, investors would be wise to take at least a portion off the table after this major run-up over the last month. I rate NUTX as a mild sell at this level.

data.title: Nebius Vs CoreWeave: Why Nebius Will Outperform
data.description:
Prelude

The neocloud industry has risen to prominence alongside commercial interest in AI. The term neocloud refers to a cloud service provider that is purpose built for AI. GPU-first data centers. Nebius Group (NASDAQ:NBIS) and CoreWeave (NASDAQ:CRWV) are two such neoclouds. In this article, I make the case that Nebius will outperform CoreWeave. Overall, Nebius’s debt‑free, vertically integrated model and favorable EV‑per‑GPU valuation make it the superior neocloud investment over CoreWeave.

Investing in either Nebius or CoreWeave represents a unilateral bet on continued strong demand for AI compute. This is a good bet. Hyperscalers have continued spending aggressively while the Trump Administration continues loosening AI-related regulations. Just today, the market applauded a flurry of announcements related to AI from the deal with the Saudis. Further, training frontier models continue to become more compute intensive:

Indeed, at a certain point we cannot simply throw more compute at models to make them smarter. The industry will need to adopt novel methods to train better models within capacity constraints. This will drive customers toward the most cost-efficient leading edge AI compute, which is Nebius. We'll return to this point later.

As the neocloud industry takes shape, the greatest opportunity comes from identifying the biggest winners early on. Both CoreWeave and Nebius are strong competitors here, but I believe Nebius stock will generate better returns over time. In this article, I rate CoreWeave a Sell and Nebius a Buy.

Why Nebius Will Outperform

Nebius stock offers a few distinct advantages over CoreWeave, in my opinion. First, the company's EU locality gives it a strong advantage in winning contracts in Europe and the Middle East due to data locality regulations like GDPR. Just today, the company was selected to build a supercomputer for the Israeli government. This EU locality will make it easier for Nebius to earn initial contracts to get started, providing sorely needed votes of customer confidence and revenue run rate to finance future GPU purchases.

CoreWeave's scale dwarfs that of Nebius: it has 250,000 GPUs currently versus Nebius' 30,000 and has 5x the Blackwell allocation. This is a double-edged sword. As Blackwell compute proliferates the market, the price of Hopper compute will continue sharply declining. These machines are still being depreciated, causing a huge overhang on earnings. Combined with CoreWeave's debt burden, the company has a much more difficult path to profitability than Nebius.

Nebius is expanding its overall GPU count by 73% with its 22,000 Blackwell GPUs while CoreWeave is expanding its count by 44% with its 110,000 Blackwell GPUs. Hopper depreciation will weigh more heavily on CoreWeave. Meanwhile, CoreWeave paid more in interest than it generated in operating income in 2024. The company's recent earnings release showed a similarly large overhang on earnings. We'll discuss that point later. Meanwhile, Nebius has no debt and earned $63m in interest income last year. Its path to profitability is much clearer than CoreWeave's.

Finally, valuation. I think a non-standard metric is best here: Enterprise Value to Blackwell capacity. To be a neocloud is to purchase the newest compute possible, operationalize it as quickly as possible, and earn as much money as it can before the next new platform is ramped. Therefore, it's best to evaluate the current enterprise value by the amount of Blackwell GPUs they have. This simplification ignores utilization and margin differences but serves as a clear value benchmark.

Nebius has an enterprise value of $6.53b using a share price of $36. It currently has about 22,000 Blackwell GPUs which it is accepting pre-orders for. This translates to a price of about $297,000 in EV per GPU.

CoreWeave's enterprise value is currently $37.6b using a share price of $62 and it has an allocation of 110,000 Blackwell GPUs. Using this proxy, you are currently paying about $342,000 in EV per GPU.

In essence, the difference in strategy (Nebius buying fewer GPUs but using cash instead of debt) allows investors to pay less per GPU for the stock. This is core as to why I believe Nebius stock offers better value than CoreWeave.

With that, let's dive a bit into each company's history, financials, and strategic approach.

Nebius

Nebius was borne out of the ashes of Yandex, a Russian tech conglomerate that managed some of the largest data centers in Europe. It is now headquartered in the Netherlands.

Former Yandex CEO Arkady Volozh, a Russian national that now lives in Tel Aviv, was placed on the EU sanctions list following the start of the Ukraine-Russia war and the company was sanctioned in the European market. Accordingly, Volozh publicly denounced the Russian invasion. Yandex assets were divested, raising over $2b in the process, that were then used to begin procuring Nvidia (NVDA) GPUs for the company's new life as Nebius.

As a result, the company's reported revenue dropped sharply from 2021 to 2022, and now sits at $117.5m. It achieved a 37% gross margin in fiscal 2024 on this revenue. For fiscal 2025, management believes it can achieve $750m-$1b in annual revenue run rate. The company has a very comfortable cash position of $2.49b as of the end of 2024 with an immaterial amount of debt. The company recently raised $700m in an oversubscribed offering that most notably included Nvidia.

The company's history is a double-edged sword. On one hand, the Russian association, even though management has distanced itself from the country and Yandex, could contribute to some reluctance from customers to sign contracts. On the other hand, the company is well-funded from the divestiture and has deep technical expertise that has allowed it to build a vertically integrated neocloud platform.

Nebius has the expertise to design its own data centers, procure its own compute, and deploy its own AI software layer. These in-house services greatly reduce the company's cost profile, allowing it to profitably offer low pricing.

Nebius' core business, AI infrastructure, currently maintains data centers in Finland, France, a pilot deployment in Kansas City, and an H200 cluster in Iceland. Most of the company's 30,000 GPUs are H200s, but it has secured a 22,000 Blackwell allocation from Nvidia. The Blackwell cluster in New Jersey is expected to be operational in 2025. This timeline is very achievable, and I expect a very positive update from management in the upcoming earnings call about this.

Nebius worked with European data center developer DataOne on this project. The CEO of DataOne shared this post on LinkedIn last week:

According to this post, the project is halfway done, and the infrastructure should be in place within the next 10 weeks. As another Seeking Alpha analyst recently pointed out, rental prices of Hopper GPUs have been declining, but I don't view this as strictly bearish for Nebius. Customers were hesitant to sign contracts for Hopper capacity in anticipation of the general rollout of Blackwell capacity across the industry. The stock will respond much more strongly to positive news about Blackwell capacity than it will respond to negative news about Hopper pricing.

Other Businesses

The core AI infrastructure business gives plenty of reason to be bullish on Nebius stock. There are strong structural tailwinds benefitting the company in this industry right now. Beyond this, Nebius has four other key points that provide a bullish backdrop for the stock in the remainder of 2025 and on. These other businesses are: Avride, Toloka, TripleTen, and ClickHouse.

Avride is an autonomous vehicle service that secured partnerships with Grubhub and Uber Eats (UBER) for autonomous food delivery services in 2024. Avride is fairly capital intensive for a non-core business, but it's done well to carve out a strong niche in the fast-growing autonomy market. It has great acquisition potential for Nebius.

Toloka is a data platform for AI training and grew sales by 140% in 2024. Toloka accelerates AI training timelines by providing important services like data labelling and red teaming for AI agents.

TripleTen is an educational tech service that helps re-skill workers for the AI era. Enrollments doubled in 2024.

Finally, the company has a 28% stake in ClickHouse, a database company in talks for a $6b funding round, valuing Nebius' stake at over $1.5b. The announcement of this funding round is what sent the stock powering forward last week.

Nebius offers direct exposure to the booming growth AI infrastructure sector and touts a set of ancillary businesses that could prove to be quite valuable over time. I particularly like the company's balance sheet, considering it has a comfortable cash pile and no debt. There won't be the overhang on earnings that high interest payments would cause, and the company can burn through its cash to accumulate GPUs before needing to rely on debt. These GPUs will become cash generative machines as the company works through the uptick in depreciation expense and then looks forward to its strategy for acquiring Nvidia's next-gen Rubin GPUs.

CoreWeave

CoreWeave has had a really strong five-year run. It transitioned from an Ethereum mining company to a neocloud after Ethereum began the shift to a proof-of-stake protocol. This was a logical transition considering it used Nvidia GPUs for its ether mining operations. It has a long-standing relationship with Nvidia and remains one of the company's leading neocloud partners.

CoreWeave garnered a lot of attention as the AI bull run found its legs. In 2022, it bought around $100m in H100s. It signed Stability AI as a customer and earned a $100m investment from Nvidia in 2023. It was valued around $2b in May of 2023 and raised $2.3b in debt using H100's as collateral in 2023 as well. It also spent $1.7b to acquire Weights & Biases, which moved the company up the value chain into AI development tools.

Just a year later, the company's valuation ballooned to $19b. It was one of the first cloud service providers to receive Blackwell racks. Finally, it planned to IPO. In its initial IPO filing, it generated 60% of its revenue from Microsoft (MSFT). The company also signed a $12b contract with OpenAI, which also allowed OpenAI to acquire $350m worth of shares during the IPO. The IPO was initially planned to raise $2.7b at a $23b valuation but later reduced the IPO size to $1.5b. The company finished 2024 with 32 data centers and more than 250,000 GPUs.

CoreWeave is the leading neocloud based on scale, GPU allocation, and customer wins. It has a deep relationship with Nvidia and exclusively offers Nvidia GPUs. This guarantees it will be able to continue getting leading edge chip allocation from the king. Despite this strong competitive positioning, the company's financial profile leaves a lot to be desired. In the company's Q1 earnings report, it reported $981m in sales, $561m in technology and infrastructure cost (which included $443m in depreciation), and $263m in interest expense. Despite the strong gross margin of 73%, depreciation expense was 45% of sales while interest expense comprised 27% of sales.

The company faced a 629% increase in technology and infrastructure costs in 2024, driven mostly by depreciation costs of Hopper GPUs. The Q1 report confirms this will continue to be a significant overhang on earnings.

The rapid growth of depreciation and interest expenses is the bear case for CoreWeave that is hidden in plain sight. These expenses aren't going anywhere. Nvidia is delivering new GPU platforms at a breakneck pace, with each successive generation offering significant performance improvements compared to the previous. The rollout of the Blackwell generation caused rental prices of Hopper compute to tumble down. This is going to be a consistent cycle for neoclouds, who will have to grapple with high depreciation costs for assets with a timestamp on their profitable lifetime. CoreWeave faces a double whammy of high depreciation costs and high interest costs while it will continue to rely on debt to acquire new GPUs.

With that, let's briefly discuss the final few key differences between Nebius and CoreWeave and I'll reiterate why I believe Nebius is set to outperform.

Key differences

Nebius built its entire platform from the ground up, while CoreWeave had to take on more debt to acquire its way up the value chain with the Weights and Biases acquisition. Considering the capital intensity of the neocloud industry and the fact that both of these companies have immaterial revenues compared to the capex required, I see Nebius as the far better option. The company has the expertise required to vertically integrate from data center and rack design up to the software layer. It has other businesses that have huge potential to accrue lots of value for shareholders.

Meanwhile, CoreWeave has several deep relationships with AI leaders. It is the leading neocloud partner for Nvidia and has close ties with Stability and OpenAI. This ensures its long-term relevance, but the company's strategy of taking debt to accumulate GPUs that quickly become obsolete makes a difficult path to profitability. Further, it had to acquire its way up the value chain (relying on more debt in the process), compared to Nebius' in-house vertical integration. By no means is CoreWeave irrelevant or not an innovator, I just believe that Nebius presents a far better opportunity at present.

Finally, on pricing. Nebius is positioning itself as the low-cost competitor while CoreWeave prefers to sign larger contracts with more premium pricing. As of May 10th, Nebius has the lowest publicly available rental cost for H200 GPUs at $2.30/hour (subject to certain terms), compared to the GetDeploying index. Nebius can offer industry leading prices profitably because its vertically integrated model provides much better unit economics. Handling data center design, OEM services, and software development internally has clear cost benefits to outsourcing these items. This should help the company overcome the initial hurdle of signing large customer contracts. However, Nebius' lack of customer wins relative to CoreWeave is the largest bearish factor in my argument. The company needs more customer wins in 2025 for the valuation to continue expanding.

Investor Takeaway

The neocloud industry is set to broadly outperform as more AI workloads shift to Blackwell chips. Despite the decline in Hopper prices, the shift in focus toward Blackwell provides a compelling setup for a refresh in neocloud growth outlooks and a rerating of valuation multiples.

Both Nebius and CoreWeave are neoclouds seeking to satiate the strong global demand for AI compute. While CoreWeave will have a much larger GPU footprint and Blackwell allocation, Nebius offers a more diversified business, more value-added services, and the agility of being debt-free.

Nebius does not yet have the brand-name recognition of CoreWeave nor the customer wins, forming the key bearish risks to my thesis, but the business is well positioned for growth. If Nebius fails to convert its Blackwell ramp into material contracts by the end of 2025, its valuation could contract materially.

However, I expect the company will either reiterate or raise the $750m-$1b expected annual revenue run rate guidance for year-end 2025 given the solid progress of its Blackwell buildout in the US. Further, the company's other businesses provide enormous upside potential that do not currently look priced into the stock. For these reasons, I expect Nebius will outperform CoreWeave.
